---
title: "05 · Multiplication as Area"
subtitle: "Two numbers, one rectangle"
lesson-id: "t1.multiply-as-area"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.add-subtract"]
unlocks: ["t1.divide-fair-share", "t1.area-perimeter-volume", "t1.intro-variables"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you compute $7 + 7 + 7 + 7$ in your head? (= 28)
2. Have you done [Lesson 4 — Addition & Subtraction, Re-grounded](../04-add-subtract/index.qmd)?
3. Do you know your "times tables" up to about $7 \times 7$ — even
   roughly?

(3) helps but is not required — multiplication tables are *summaries* of
what you will derive in this lesson.
:::

## Touch

Drag the sliders to set the **width** and **height** of a rectangle. Count
the small squares inside.

```{ojs}
viewof width = Inputs.range([1, 10], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "Width:"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof height = Inputs.range([1, 8], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "Height:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const cellSize = 36, gap = 2, pad = 30;
  const W = pad * 2 + 10 * (cellSize + gap);
  const H = pad * 2 + 8 * (cellSize + gap) + 30;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:560px; background:#fafafa; border:1px solid #e0e0e0; border-radius:6px;");
  const product = width * height;

  for (let row = 0; row < height; row++) {
    for (let col = 0; col < width; col++) {
      svg.append("rect")
        .attr("x", pad + col * (cellSize + gap))
        .attr("y", pad + row * (cellSize + gap))
        .attr("width", cellSize).attr("height", cellSize)
        .attr("fill", "#90caf9").attr("stroke", "#2c5f7c").attr("stroke-width", 1);
    }
  }
  // Count label
  svg.append("text")
    .attr("x", pad + width * (cellSize + gap) / 2)
    .attr("y", pad + height * (cellSize + gap) + 22)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "16").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#1f2933")
    .text(`${width} × ${height} = ${product} squares`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

That number — the count of small squares — is **width × height**. We call
it **the product**. Multiplication is the recipe for that count.

## Wonder

1. If you swap the width and the height (4 × 3 vs. 3 × 4), does the count
   change? Why or why not?
2. What does $0 \times 5$ look like as a rectangle? What is the count?
3. The widget caps at 10 × 8. Could you draw 100 × 100? How many squares
   would that be?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Set the slider to width 4, height 3. Count the squares — first by *rows*,
then by *columns*. Did you get the same number? Should you?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes — 12 either way.** Counting by rows: 3 rows of 4 = $4 + 4 + 4 = 12$.
Counting by columns: 4 columns of 3 = $3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 12$. They must
agree, because *the squares didn't move*. This is why
$$3 \times 4 = 4 \times 3.$$
Multiplication is **commutative**, just like addition. The picture proves
it.
</details>

### Problem 2

Compute $6 \times 7$ without looking up a times table. Use repeated
addition.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$6 \times 7$ means "six rows of seven" or equivalently "seven rows of six".
Either repeated addition works. The smaller number of additions is
faster.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 7 = 42$, or equivalently $6 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 =
42$. **$6 \times 7 = 42$.**

Times tables are just *memorised* answers to these counts. Once memorised,
they save the repeated additions — but the rectangle is always there
beneath the table.
</details>

### Problem 3

A floor tile is a square 1 metre on each side. A room measures **8 m by
5 m**. How many tiles does it take to cover the floor?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is *literally* the rectangle picture above, scaled up.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$8 \times 5 = 40$ tiles. The "small squares" of the widget are now
1-metre tiles. **Area is multiplication made visible.**
</details>

### Problem 4

Imagine a rectangle whose width is **23** and whose height is **5**. What
is the count?

You don't have the widget for that — too big. But you can split the
rectangle into two pieces.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Split the width 23 into 20 + 3. Now you have two smaller rectangles:
20 × 5 and 3 × 5.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$23 \times 5 = (20 + 3) \times 5 = 20 \times 5 + 3 \times 5 = 100 + 15 = 115$.

You just used the **distributive law** without anyone naming it. Picture:
the 23 × 5 rectangle, split by a vertical line, becomes two rectangles
sitting side by side. Their squares add up to the same total. We will
name this property properly in
[Lesson 11: Variables](../11-intro-variables/index.qmd).
</details>

### Problem 5

What does **$0 \times 5$** look like as a rectangle? What is the count?

What does **$1 \times 5$** look like?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Width 0 means... no width.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$0 \times 5$:** the rectangle has no width. It collapses to a line
segment with zero squares. $0 \times 5 = 0$.

**$1 \times 5$:** a single column of 5 squares. $1 \times 5 = 5$.

These give us two important rules:

- **Multiplying by 0 always gives 0** (the rectangle collapses).
- **Multiplying by 1 leaves the number unchanged** (one row of $n$ squares
  is just $n$ squares). 1 is the *multiplicative identity*.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
Around 1800 BCE, the **Babylonians** were doing serious multiplication
with clay-tablet *times tables* — and not for ones through twelves, but
for *every number up to 60* (their base). Some of the surviving tablets
also contain *reciprocal* tables (used to turn a division into a
multiplication), making them the world's first known multiplication
worksheets. Four thousand years on, schoolchildren still memorise the
same rectangles — just in base 10.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
Multiplication isn't repeated addition with extra steps — it's a
rectangle. Width times height. Once you see the rectangle, you can swap
the sides for free, slice it for clever splits, and watch it collapse
when one side hits zero. The picture survives every later number system
the algebra doesn't.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

For two whole numbers $a$ and $b$, the **product** $a \times b$ is the
total count of small squares in a rectangle of width $a$ and height $b$.

Equivalently, $a \times b$ is "$b$ added to itself $a$ times" — the
**repeated-addition** view.

Multiplication is **commutative**: $a \times b = b \times a$.
Multiplying by **0 gives 0**. Multiplying by **1 leaves the number
unchanged**.

:::

> *To multiply is to count the squares of a rectangle;*
> *Width times height — a single number for the whole.*
> *Swap the sides — the count is the same.*
> *Zero collapses; one preserves.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $24 \times 6$.

A competent solver splits the 24 into place-value chunks: $24 = 20 + 4$.

1. $20 \times 6 = 120$ (because $2 \times 6 = 12$, and we are working in
   tens).
2. $4 \times 6 = 24$.
3. Sum: $120 + 24 = 144$.

**Answer: 144.** Picture: a rectangle 24 wide, 6 tall, sliced into a
20 × 6 piece and a 4 × 6 piece. The two pieces together have 144 squares.

This is exactly what the standard "long multiplication" algorithm does on
paper — split by place value, multiply each chunk, add the partial
products.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Without a calculator: $7 \times 8$, $\ 9 \times 6$, $\ 3 \times
12$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7 \times 8 = 56$, $\ 9 \times 6 = 54$, $\ 3 \times 12 = 36$.
</details>

**W2.** What is $0 \times 9837$? What is $1 \times 9837$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0$ and $9837$. The size of the other number doesn't matter; the rules of
multiplying by 0 and 1 are absolute.
</details>

**W3.** A garden bed is **4 m wide and 9 m long**. A bag of fertiliser
covers **1 square metre**. How many bags do you need?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4 \times 9 = 36$ bags.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Compute $36 \times 7$ by splitting 36 into 30 + 6.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$30 \times 7 = 210$, $\ 6 \times 7 = 42$, sum = **252**.
</details>

**P2.** A bookshelf has **5 shelves**. Each shelf holds **8 hardcovers**
or **15 paperbacks**. If three shelves are filled with hardcovers and two
with paperbacks, how many books total?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two multiplications, then an addition.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Hardcovers: $3 \times 8 = 24$. Paperbacks: $2 \times 15 = 30$. Total
$24 + 30 = 54$ books.
</details>

**P3.** Compute $25 \times 4$ in your head. Then compute $25 \times 16$
by reusing what you just found.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$16 = 4 \times 4$. So $25 \times 16 = (25 \times 4) \times 4$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$25 \times 4 = 100$. Then $25 \times 16 = 100 \times 4 = 400$.

You just used the **associative** property: $(25 \times 4) \times 4 =
25 \times (4 \times 4)$. The brackets can move freely. Multiplying in
the easiest order is a powerful mental-arithmetic trick.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A computer screen is **1920 pixels wide** and **1080 pixels tall**
(a "Full HD" screen). How many pixels in total?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is a rectangle picture again — width × height. Use round-number
splitting: $1920 = 2000 - 80$, or $1920 \times 1000 + 1920 \times 80$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1920 \times 1080$. Split: $1920 \times 1000 = 1{,}920{,}000$, and
$1920 \times 80 = 153{,}600$. Sum: **2,073,600 pixels**, often quoted as
"about 2 megapixels".

Every image, every video frame, every game render is exactly this
rectangle — width times height — except instead of counting squares, the
computer is choosing a colour for each one. Multiplication is the heart
of every screen.
</details>

**S2.** Why do you think mathematicians sometimes write multiplication
*without* the × sign — for example, $3a$ to mean $3 \times a$, or $(x+1)
(x-2)$? When would the × sign cause confusion?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at $x \times y$ and ask: is that *one* thing or *three* things
written together?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The × sign looks confusingly like the letter $x$. Once we start using
**variables** (letter-numbers — see [Lesson 11](../11-intro-variables/index.qmd)),
$x \times y$ becomes ambiguous. The convention "just write the symbols
next to each other" — *juxtaposition* — solves it: $3a$, $xy$,
$(x+1)(x-2)$.

This is one of those small notational decisions whose effects ripple
through every later lesson in algebra, calculus, and beyond.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Multiplication is just repeated addition."** It is — for whole
   numbers. But once we extend to fractions, decimals, and negatives, the
   *rectangle picture* survives where the *repeated-addition picture*
   breaks down. ("How do you add 0.5 to itself 0.3 times?") The geometric
   view is the more general one.
2. **"$a \times b$ is bigger than both $a$ and $b$."** True only when both
   are bigger than 1. $0.5 \times 4 = 2$ is *smaller* than 4. Lesson 7
   (fractions) and Lesson 8 (decimals) will revisit this.
3. **"Multiplication tables are something to memorise blindly."** The
   tables are *summaries* of the rectangles. Knowing why $7 \times 8 = 56$
   (because seven rows of eight squares = 56 squares) means you can
   reconstruct the answer if you forget it.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **[Multiplication](/glossary.qmd#multiplication) is the area of a
   rectangle: width times height, counted as small squares.**
2. **You can swap the sides ([commutative](/glossary.qmd#commutative)) and
   slice the rectangle into chunks
   ([distributive](/glossary.qmd#distributive)) to make hard products easy.**
3. **Zero collapses the rectangle to nothing; one leaves it unchanged —
   and the rectangle picture keeps working when fractions, decimals, and
   matrices arrive later.**

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you draw the picture for $5 \times 7$ on a scrap of paper, and
   say in one sentence why $5 \times 7 = 7 \times 5$?
2. Can you compute $36 \times 7$ in your head using place-value
   splitting?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

- **3 yes:** ready for **[Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **P3** and **S1** — those exercise the splitting
  technique.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover, Problems 1 and 4* — the rectangle
  picture and the splitting trick are the lesson.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 4 — Addition & Subtraction](../04-add-subtract/index.qmd)** —
  multiplication is *built from* repeated addition; computing
  partial-product sums uses the carry algorithm.

### What this unlocks

- **[Division as Fair Share](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)** —
  division undoes multiplication: "given the area, find a side length."
- **[Area, Perimeter, Volume](../13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)** —
  the rectangle picture *is* area; we extend to other shapes and to 3D.
- **[Variables](../11-intro-variables/index.qmd)** — distributivity
  (*Discover P4*) and juxtaposition (*Stretch S2*) need names.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development & graphics.** Every screen is width × height pixels;
every texture is a multiplication of dimensions; every image-processing
operation is a sweep over that rectangle. Matrix multiplication — the
engine of every 3D transform — is *rectangles of rectangles*, repeated
many times.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** A neural network layer is, computationally, a
huge matrix multiplication: a weight rectangle multiplied by an input
vector. Modern AI training runs are measured in *trillions of
multiplications per second*. The operation is the same one you just met;
the scale is what's new.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Pricing (price × quantity), area (length × width),
energy (power × time), travel (speed × time) — multiplication is the
operation that combines two units into one. Understanding it as a
rectangle is the difference between manipulating symbols and
*seeing* what is going on.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the rectangle is the canonical
  Dienes-block picture for multiplication), Boole (the swap-the-sides
  question surfaces commutativity before it is named), Russian school
  (the distributive law in P4 falls out of *splitting* a rectangle),
  Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse), Better Explained (the "Aha!"
  framing is *area is multiplication, not the other way around*).
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
